description: A tapping foot and a thought that will not settle can both be 'wipha'. The word covers a bodily urge to move and inward agitation. A person can be motionless yet restless, which keeps 'wipha' distinct from 'moesha' (still).
articulatory_notes: Round the lips and raise the back of the tongue for voiced 'w', then move forward and unround into high-front 'i'. Bring the lips close together for voiceless bilabial 'ph'. Air passes between them before the mouth opens into central 'a'. Stress falls on 'wi'.
usage_notes: 'wipha miona' is a restless person and 'wipha korua' a restless heart. The word can describe visible behaviour or an inward state; 'shena' (calm) gives the low-agitation counterpart.

semantic_domains.physical: recurrent movement or an urge to move
